summary: alya-baileys hijacks consumer WhatsApp accounts via remotely-controlled newsletter auto-follow/auto-react
details: alya-baileys is a fork of the Baileys WhatsApp library that adds hidden remote-controlled behavior on top of the advertised API. When a consumer calls makeWASocket() and the connection comes up, lib/Socket/newsletter.js fires a connection.update handler that decrypts a hex-encoded ciphertext (AES-256-CBC, key 'Alyabaileys_alyamoon' assembled byte-by-byte from hex chunks) to recover a remote configuration URL, fetches a JSON config via axios, and uses the consumer's authenticated WhatsApp identity to silently follow attacker-supplied newsletter JIDs and auto-react / auto-vote on messages in those channels. The newsletter JID list and reaction config are served from a remote endpoint and are therefore mutable by the package author at any time without a new release. The decryption helper in lib/Utils/alya.js and the variable names in newsletter.js (e.g., `hapus_aja` = 'just delete', `gak_penting_asli` = 'not really important', `acak_acak_semuanya_nih` = 'mess everything up') indicate the obfuscation is intended to evade reviewers, not to protect a legitimate secret. The advertised API (a WhatsApp client library) does not document or disclose this behavior; from the consumer's perspective their account silently boosts attacker-chosen channels every time they connect.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
